parents.father_research_notes: Lt. Samuel Stiles of Windsor CT

bio.notes: Hannah Stiles married her cousin Ashbell Stiles. She was daughter of Lt. Samuel Stiles and Mary Phelps of Windsor CT. Same surname before and after marriage.

parents.father_research_notes: Lt. Samuel Stiles of Windsor CT

bio.notes: Hannah Stiles married her cousin Ashbell Stiles. She was daughter of Lt. Samuel Stiles and Mary Phelps of Windsor CT. Same surname before and after marriage.

military_service.notes: Per The Stiles Family in America (BIB008) entry #230: served in the Revolution and was at Horse Neck from May 1781 to March 1782. Horse Neck (Greenwich CT) was an active Revolutionary War outpost under occasional British raid throughout the war.
Served in the Revolution at Horse Neck (now Greenwich CT, on the western frontier facing British-held New York), from May 1781 to March 1782.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited mansion, lost all endorsing for friend
narrative_blocks.body: Ashbel Stiles inherited the family mansion and a very comfortable property from his father in North Haven Connecticut. But in an evil hour he endorsed for a friend and lost his all. Soon after he removed to Windsor Connecticut, and thence to Norwich Massachusetts — now Huntington — near his daughter Ruth Stiles Ellis.

narrative_blocks.header: Hannah died Norwich one month before Ashbel did
narrative_blocks.body: After fifty-one years of marriage, Ashbel and his cousin-wife Hannah Stiles both died at Norwich (now Huntington) Massachusetts in autumn 1810 — both at age seventy-five. Hannah went first in September; Ashbel followed in October, approximately one month later.

narrative_blocks.header: Son thrown from a horse, killed in Jamaica
narrative_blocks.body: His son Benjamin Stiles died at Jamaica in the West Indies from injuries sustained being thrown from a horse. The Stiles Family in America entry 230 records the death without further circumstance — a son lost an ocean from home to a single fall.

narrative_blocks.header: Horse Neck garrison 1781 after financial ruin
narrative_blocks.body: Forty-six at enlistment, he came to Revolutionary militia service well after most of his cohort. Financial ruin from endorsing for a friend had already emptied the family mansion he had inherited from his father.

bio.notes: Named "Esther Hooker" — explicitly honoring grandmother Esther Hooker Stiles (H00092). The full given name preserves both the family name and the Hooker identity.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entry 461
Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895), Samuel Stiles entry 233
Historic Northampton, Gare Collection: Samuel Stiles silvermaking entry

notable.primary_url_label: Historic Northampton: Samuel Stiles, the Gare Collection of Silvermaking and Watch Repair Tools

military_service.notes: Served in a company formed in Windsor CT during the War of 1812 with Britain. Stationed at Fort Trumbull (New London CT) in February 1813.

narrative_blocks.header: War of 1812 private, stationed Fort Trumbull
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Stiles served as a private in a company formed in Windsor CT during the War of 1812 with Britain, stationed at Fort Trumbull (New London) in February 1813. He was 50 years old at the time — old for a militiaman — but the Connecticut coast was being threatened by British naval blockade, and middle-aged men were drawn into local defense.

narrative_blocks.header: Died on his thirty-ninth wedding anniversary
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Stiles married Hannah Ellsworth in Windsor on October 15, 1787; he died at Windsor on October 15, 1826 — exactly thirty-nine years later, on their wedding anniversary. Hannah survived him by only fifteen months, dying at Chicopee MA in January 1828.

narrative_blocks.header: Three states across forty years of marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel and Hannah's life together threaded across three states: their first three children were born in Northampton MA (1788-1791); the next six in Chester MA (1793-1802); and they finally returned to Samuel's native Windsor CT where he died in 1826. The pattern reflects the era's flexible Connecticut-Massachusetts borderland economy.

narrative_blocks.header: Northampton silversmith — surviving spoons in Deerfield
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Stiles opened a clock and watch shop on Shop Row in Northampton in 1785 — a few rods south of the Court-House — and worked as a clockmaker, watchmaker, jeweler, and silversmith for six years before partnering with Jedidiah Baldwin in July 1791. Two silver spoons bearing his 'SS' maker's mark survive in the collection of Historic Deerfield. The partnership dissolved within a year and Samuel left Northampton for Chester MA in 1792.

career.organization: Samuel Stiles shop, Shop Row, Northampton MA
career.notes: Opened shop in Northampton 1785, a few rods south of the Court-House. Advertised in Springfield Hampshire Herald July 27, 1785. Made silver including spoons with maker's mark 'SS' (two examples in Historic Deerfield collection). Took apprentices Joel Brown and Horace Clark, c.1789-1793.

Partnership with Jedidiah Baldwin (1769-1849), formed July 20, 1791 in shop nearly opposite the Meeting-house in Northampton — clock and watch business, jewelry, all branches of goldsmith's business. Baldwin had served apprenticeship to Thomas Harland of Norwich CT. Partnership lasted approximately one year before Samuel left Northampton.

parents.father_research_notes: Of Windsor CT. Not in JSON.
parents.mother_research_notes: Of Windsor CT. Maiden name not documented in EH 461. Not in JSON.

narrative_blocks.header: Windsor Ellsworth family — Chief Justice was kin
narrative_blocks.body: She was a daughter of Jonathan Ellsworth of Windsor Connecticut, of the same Windsor Ellsworth family that produced Oliver Ellsworth (1745-1807) — third Chief Justice of the United States and co-author of the Connecticut Compromise. Specific kinship between Hannah's father and Oliver has not yet been documented but the Windsor Ellsworth naming patterns place them in the same kin network.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at Chicopee Massachusetts after husband Samuel
narrative_blocks.body: She survived her husband Samuel Stiles by fifteen months — Samuel died at Chicopee Massachusetts in October 1826, Hannah followed there on 12 January 1828. The Connecticut-born couple's migration to Chicopee tracked their daughter Delia, who lived there.

narrative_blocks.header: Longest-lived of nine, a widow for decades
narrative_blocks.body: Delia Stiles Van Horn outlived all eight of her Stiles siblings and survived her husband Ruel, a Chicopee farmer, by twenty-four years. She lived to eighty-two.

narrative_blocks.header: Her son was Lincoln’s consul at Marseilles
narrative_blocks.body: Her son George W. Van Horn was appointed United States Consul at Marseilles by President Lincoln and served through 1866. He later founded a newspaper in Iowa and wrote novels.

narrative_blocks.header: Drowned in Lake Erie at twenty-five, June 1818
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Stiles drowned in Lake Erie on June 24, 1818, age 25 — only eight years before his father, his namesake, would die at Windsor. Lake Erie in 1818 was the gateway to the Connecticut Western Reserve where his second cousin Leonard Tuttle would migrate eighteen years later; whether young Samuel was traveling to the Reserve when he drowned is undocumented but historically plausible.

cross_connections.display_label: Second cousins via Esther Hooker, both migrated to Ohio
First cousins, sons of brothers Job and Samuel Stiles

narrative_blocks.header: Migrated to Ohio, like his second cousin Leonard
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Stiles was born in Chester MA in 1795 and died in Ohio — joining the same Connecticut Western Reserve migration that brought his second cousin Leonard Tuttle to Medina County by 1836. Both descend from Esther Hooker Stiles, the gen-3 ancestor whose family produced two parallel mid-1800s Ohio settlements.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Phelps' carries his paternal grandmother Hannah Stiles Stiles's mother's family — Mary Phelps married Lt. Samuel Stiles, and her surname descended to her great-grandson as a middle name marker.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a year, no issue, both spouses dead
narrative_blocks.body: Amelia Stiles married William Welch on August 14, 1821 in Massachusetts. He died in 1822; she followed on December 30, 1822 at age 20. They left no children.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin who survived, two marriages, one son
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Stiles was born June 10, 1802 in Chester MA — the same day as her twin Amelia, who would die at twenty. Julia married Calvin Cooley in 1823, the year after Amelia's death, then remarried Sidney Crawford in 1856; one son is documented from the second marriage. Death date not in EH 461.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entry 462
Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895), entry [-34] of Job Stiles

career.notes: Shoemaker by trade (EH 462 + Stiles book). The Stiles family resided in Enfield CT for about ten years (c.1810-1821) where Job died in 1813, then his widow returned to Windsor.

burial.plot_notes: Palisado Cemetery, Windsor CT — buried 'on one stone' with wife Mary Drake Stiles.

narrative_blocks.header: Windsor shoemaker, brother of silversmith Samuel
narrative_blocks.body: Job Stiles was a shoemaker by trade, working in Windsor CT and later Enfield CT. His older brother Samuel Stiles was a Northampton silversmith and clockmaker — together they represent two New England craft trades within the same Stiles family generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-eight, buried with Mary in Windsor
narrative_blocks.body: Job died at Windsor CT on April 13, 1813 at age 48, during the family's roughly decade-long Enfield CT residence (c.1810-1821). His widow Mary Drake Stiles survived him by twenty-six years, dying March 14, 1839 at age 77. They were buried together in the Old Windsor Graveyard with their names inscribed on a single stone.

burial.plot_notes: Palisado Cemetery, Windsor CT — 'on one stone' with husband Job Stiles.

parents.father_research_notes: Of Windsor CT. Not in JSON.
parents.mother_research_notes: Of Windsor CT, of the Griswold family — middle name of grandson Ezra Griswold Stiles (HD4158) carries this lineage. Not in JSON.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty-six years widowed, buried three children
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Drake Stiles was widowed at fifty-one in 1813 when her husband Job died. She lived another twenty-six years in Windsor, dying at seventy-seven in 1839. She buried three of her nine children: infant Mary in September 1786 (age 5 months), Laura in September 1810 (age 21), and Ezra Griswold lost at sea around 1819 (presumed dead at 28).

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entry 462
Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895)

narrative_blocks.header: Baptized September 25, died two days later
narrative_blocks.body: Mary was the first child of Job Stiles and Mary Drake. She was born April 24, 1786 and died September 27, 1786 at age five months — two days after her September 25 baptism. The pattern suggests the baptism was deathbed: she was likely already failing when the family rushed her to the font.

narrative_blocks.header: Ninety-one years in Windsor, never married
narrative_blocks.body: Lucretia Stiles lived ninety-one years in Windsor CT, never married. She and her younger sister Mary II(1797-1887, lived 90 years) together represented the unmarried Stiles sisters of their generation. Lucretia kept her brother Ezra Griswold's 1814 War of 1812 captivity letter in her possession at Windsor — its survival and reproduction in the 1895 Stiles Family in America came through her.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-one, three years before her father
narrative_blocks.body: Laura Stiles died September 16, 1810 in Windsor CT at age 21 — the first adult-age sibling loss in Job and Mary Drake Stiles's family. Her father Job followed her into the Old Windsor Graveyard three years later in April 1813. Her brother Ezra Griswold was lost at sea around 1819, presumed dead at 28 — the second adult-age sibling loss.

career.notes: Left Windsor home Oct 12, 1812 age 21 for seafaring career. Two-year wartime captivity 1813-1814 under British Royal Navy. Returned to seafaring after release. Lost at sea c.1819 — never returned home, never heard from again, presumed dead.

military_service.notes: Captured by British Royal Navy at Lisbon April-May 1813 while serving on the brig Jane Bants under British license. Held on HMS Cornua, then HMS San Joseph (112 guns), then prison-ship Minerva at Gibraltar, then transferred to prison-ship Hector at Plymouth England. Petitioned the Board of Admiralty in London for release; granted in approximately August 1813 on condition of not serving against England during the remainder of the war.

naming_inspiration.description: First name 'Ezra' honors Ezra Stiles (HD0346, 1727-1795, 7th President of Yale, his great-half-uncle through paternal grandfather Ashbel Stiles's half-brother Isaac Stiles's first marriage). Ezra Stiles died May 12, 1795 when Ezra Griswold was nearly 4 years old — close enough in time for a directly-known namesake.
Middle name 'Griswold' carries his mother Mary Drake's mother's maiden name — the Griswold family of Windsor CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Captured at Lisbon, held on three British prison-ships
narrative_blocks.body: Ezra Griswold Stiles was a 21-year-old Windsor CT seaman when the War of 1812 caught him in foreign waters. After his US merchant ship was blockaded at Norfolk in 1813, he walked 500 miles to New York and shipped out again — only to be captured by British naval forces at Lisbon. He was held first on HMS Cornua, then transferred to the 112-gun three-decker HMS San Joseph, then imprisoned at Gibraltar on the prison-ship Minerva, then at Plymouth on the prison-ship Hector — see notable_story for the full narrative.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost at sea 1819, never heard again
narrative_blocks.body: After his 1813 release from British captivity, Ezra Griswold Stiles continued seafaring through Newfoundland, Spain, Italy, England, and likely South America. He never returned home to Windsor. Around 1819 he was lost at sea — his family at Windsor never heard from him again, and his sister Mary II kept his 1814 letter from the prison-ship era as the family's last written contact.

sources.label: Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895), entry 415 with quoted letter
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entry 1205

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins, sons of brothers Job and Samuel Stiles
Both Hooker descendants died in Waterville Lucas County Ohio

narrative_blocks.header: Migrated to Waterville Lucas County Ohio
narrative_blocks.body: Hylas Stiles was born in Windsor CT (1793, same day-of-year as his sister Laura), grew up through the family's Enfield CT period, and migrated to Waterville in Lucas County, Ohio at some point before his 1871 death. The migration places him in the Connecticut-to-Ohio settlement pattern that brought several Hooker descendants westward — his second cousin Leonard Tuttle settled in Medina County Ohio by 1836, his first cousin Henry Stiles also died in Ohio, and Hooker descendant Nathaniel Eggleston Hooker died in the same Waterville OH fifteen years after Hylas.

narrative_blocks.header: One of three Stiles cousins to migrate Ohio
narrative_blocks.body: Hylas Stiles was one of three Esther Hooker Stiles descendants to migrate to Ohio in the same generation. His first cousin Henry Stiles(son of his uncle Samuel) also died in Ohio. His second cousin Leonard Tuttle(grandson of his great-aunt Keziah Stiles via Kezia Munson Tuttle) settled in Medina County Ohio by 1836.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entry 1206
Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895), entry 416

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Hartford CT — buried with husband Frederick Augustus Hale and several children including James Woodbridge Hale, Olive Rockwell Hale, and Edith Stiles Hale.

narrative_blocks.header: Married thirty, lost three of six children
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Stiles married Frederick A Hale of Glastonbury CT on April 7, 1825 at age 30 — late by the era's standards. She bore six children over the next fourteen years; three of them died young (an infant daughter in 1826, James at 10 months in 1830, Alfred at 7 months in 1832). The three who survived to adulthood were Charles Frederick Hale MD (1827, Panama Railroad chief medical officer), James Woodbridge Hale (1833), and Cornelius Stiles Hale (1839, died San Antonio TX 1876 unmarried).

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entry 1207
Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895), entry 417

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her older sister Mary (HD4155, 1786-1786) who died at five months — the classical New England colonial pattern of reusing a deceased child's name. Mary II's parents thus named two daughters Mary; the first lived 5 months, the second lived 90 years.

narrative_blocks.header: Ninety years unmarried, kept brother Ezra's letter
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Stiles II lived ninety years in Windsor CT, never married. She and her older sister Lucretia(91 years unmarried) together kept the family's Windsor home and memory through the nineteenth century. Per the Stiles book, the original 1814 letter from their brother Ezra Griswold describing his War of 1812 captivity was 'in the possession of his sister, Miss Mary Stiles, of Windsor' — making her the keeper of the family's most epic surviving artifact.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entry 1208
Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895), entry 424

burial.plot_notes: Palisado Cemetery, Windsor CT — buried with siblings and parents Job + Mary Drake Stiles.

narrative_blocks.header: Middle name Griswold for maternal grandmother
narrative_blocks.body: Eliza G Stiles's middle initial 'G.' on her Palisado Cemetery gravestone almost certainly stands for Griswold — carrying her maternal grandmother Mary Griswold Drake's family name into a second sibling, after her older brother Ezra Griswold Stiles. The Griswold name passed twice into Job and Mary Drake's children, a pattern of explicit maternal-grandmother memorialization. Eliza was eight when her father Job died in 1813 and lived through the Civil War until 1862, age 58, in her native Windsor.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entry 1209

burial.plot_notes: Derby Center Cemetery, Orleans County Vermont — per FindAGrave #235170328. Died a year before husband Elisha relocated the surviving family to Montreal.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-five, before family's Montreal move
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Stiles Lyman died February 25, 1814 at Derby Vermont, age forty-five — one year before her widower Elisha relocated the surviving family to Montreal where they would remain for thirteen years. She had borne ten children over twenty years; two had died in infancy (Ashbel Stiles in 1796 and Horace in 1798) and the youngest, Henry, was only four months old when she died. Her three eldest surviving sons would become the founders of Lyman Brothers, the largest pharmaceutical wholesaler in Canada.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of the Lyman drug dynasty
narrative_blocks.body: Three of Hannah Stiles Lyman's sons — William (Montreal druggist), Benjamin (co-founder of Lyman Brothers, the largest pharmaceutical company in Canada), and Henry (Montreal lawyer and Lyman Brothers partner) — together built the Lyman drug empire across Montreal and Toronto. Her grandson Henry Herbert Lyman would later bequeath his entomology collection to McGill University, founding what became the Lyman Entomological Museum.

parents.father_research_notes: Of Northampton MA. Elisha Sr. left his son Elisha Jr. the family homestead; Elisha Jr. exchanged it with his brother Theodore and left Northampton.
parents.mother_research_notes: Of Northampton MA, maiden name Janes.

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal, Outremont Montréal — per FA #251558309. Family memorial commemorates Elisha together with all three of his wives.

career.notes: Per EH bio: farmer who settled successively at Conway MA, Derby VT, Montreal (where he resided from 1815 to 1828, thirteen years), Sunderland MA, and Champlain NY. Inherited the family homestead from his father Elisha Sr. but exchanged it with his brother Theodore and left Northampton.

narrative_blocks.header: Six-state migration across forty years, three wives
narrative_blocks.body: Elisha Lyman Jr. married three times — Hannah Stiles in 1793 (died 1814), Thankful Hunt the same year Hannah died, and Sarah Beaumont in 1837 the same year Thankful died. He moved his family across an extraordinary geographic arc: Northampton Massachusetts to Conway Massachusetts, then Derby Vermont, then thirteen years in Montreal Canada from 1815 to 1828, then Sunderland Massachusetts, finally Champlain New York where he died in 1844 at age seventy-four.

narrative_blocks.header: Memorialized with all three wives in Montreal
narrative_blocks.body: Elisha Lyman Jr. died at Champlain New York February 21, 1844 at age seventy-four. He was buried at Cimetière Mont-Royal in Montreal — the city where his second wife Thankful had died in 1837, and where his sons William, Benjamin, and Henry had settled to build the Lyman Brothers drug empire. A family memorial at Mount Royal commemorates Elisha together with all three of his wives.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Royal Cemetery Montreal — Montreal druggist, eldest son.

narrative_blocks.header: Eldest son, Montreal druggist before the Lyman dynasty
narrative_blocks.body: William Lyman was the eldest of Hannah Stiles and Elisha Lyman's ten children, born April 9, 1794 at Conway Massachusetts. He moved with the family to Montreal in 1815 at age twenty-one and established himself as a Montreal druggist — the first of his brothers to enter the pharmaceutical trade in Canada. He died at Champlain New York September 7, 1857 and was buried at Mount Royal Cemetery Montreal.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for maternal grandfather Ashbel Stiles of Windsor CT — the first sibling to bear both his grandfather's first and surname.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived seven days, namesake of grandfather
narrative_blocks.body: Ashbel Stiles Lyman was born May 27, 1796 at Conway Massachusetts and died June 3, 1796 — lived seven days. He was the second of Hannah Stiles and Elisha Lyman's children, named for his maternal grandfather Ashbel Stiles of Windsor Connecticut. His death came two years before another infant brother, Horace, died at Derby Vermont in April 1798.

narrative_blocks.header: First child born in Derby, lived weeks
narrative_blocks.body: Horace Lyman was born April 11, 1798 at Derby Vermont — the first of Hannah and Elisha's children born after the family's move from Conway Massachusetts. He died in April 1798, only weeks old. He was the second infant death for Hannah and Elisha; their previous son Ashbel Stiles had died at Conway in 1796.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Montreal mayor who died caring for immigrants
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Lyman married John Easton Mills January 14, 1826 in Montreal. Her husband became Mayor of Montreal and died in 1847 of typhus contracted while personally caring for Irish famine immigrants in the city's fever sheds. She survived him by thirty-four years, eventually dying at Norwood near London England at age eighty-one in 1881.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sons all died in single 1841 year
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Lyman Mills lost three of her sons in 1841: George at age six, Edwin at age two, and Albert in infancy. The pattern suggests a single epidemic or catastrophic event swept through the Mills household that year. She would lose her husband John Easton Mills to typhus six years later, leaving her with three daughters who all married into the Montreal mercantile elite.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Stiles carries the maternal grandfather's surname — second of three Lyman siblings (with Edwin Stiles and Frederick Stiles in the next generation) to bear it.

narrative_blocks.header: Died unmarried in Illinois at forty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: Elisha Stiles Lyman migrated from Vermont and Quebec to Ottawa Illinois at some point in the late 1840s — the only one of Hannah and Elisha's ten children to settle in the American West. He died unmarried at Ottawa Illinois March 12, 1852 at age forty-eight. His middle name Stiles carried the maternal grandfather's surname, a pattern repeated in his younger brother Edwin Stiles.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Stiles carries the maternal grandfather's surname — sibling pattern with Elisha Stiles Lyman before him and Frederick Stiles Lyman in the next generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Died of cholera Montreal 1832, age twenty-six
narrative_blocks.body: Edwin Stiles Lyman died of cholera at Montreal September 22, 1832 at age twenty-six — during the first major North American cholera pandemic, which killed roughly four thousand Montrealers, or six percent of the city's population, in a single summer. He was unmarried. His death came seventeen years after the Lyman family had settled in Montreal in 1815 and four years after their 1828 departure for Sunderland Massachusetts — Edwin may have remained in Montreal when his father moved south.

HD0582.prominent_name_reason: Benjamin Lyman (1810-1878) — Thomas Hooker's great-great-great-grandson, Canadian chemist and drug manufacturer. Co-founded Lyman Bros & Co, the largest pharmaceutical company in Canada. Captain, 5th Company Montreal Rifles during the Lower Canada Rebellion of 1838.

notable.notable_blurb: Co-founded Lyman Brothers, largest Canadian pharmaceutical wholesaler

narrative_blocks.header: Built largest pharmaceutical company in Canada
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Lyman was born June 11, 1810 at Derby Vermont, the ninth of Hannah Stiles and Elisha Lyman's ten children. He co-founded Lyman Brothers and Company — the largest pharmaceutical wholesaler in Canada — with his older brother William and younger brother Henry. He served as Captain of the Fifth Company Montreal Militia and died at Toronto December 5, 1878.

narrative_blocks.header: A rifle captain in the Rebellion
narrative_blocks.body: Before the drug-wholesaling fortune, Lyman captained the 5th Company of the Montreal Rifles through the Lower Canada Rebellion of 1838. He lies at Mount Royal.

notable.notable_blurb: Quebec lawyer, Lyman Brothers Montreal drug merchant

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Henry Lyman (1813-1897), Montreal lawyer and Lyman Brothers partner.

military_service.notes: Some military service in Montreal per EH bio — specific war and dates undocumented.

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal Montreal — buried with wife Mary Corse Lyman.

education.notes: Graduated McGill College, Montreal — per EH bio. McGill founded 1821 in Montreal; Henry would have attended in the 1830s.
Further study at a college in England per EH bio — specific institution undocumented.

education.school_name: Bar of the Province of Quebec
education.notes: Admitted to practice law in the Province of Quebec.

career.notes: Joined the family drug firm in 1829 at age 15-16. Partner in Lyman Brothers — the largest pharmaceutical wholesaler in Canada — alongside his older brothers William and Benjamin. Listed as Drug Merchant in St Antoine Ward Montreal in the 1881 census.
Admitted to practice law in the Province of Quebec; partner in Lyman, Class & Co. law firm. Distinct from Lyman Brothers drug firm.
Served on Montreal City Council as Superintendent of West Ward.

narrative_blocks.header: McGill graduate, Quebec lawyer, Lyman Brothers partner
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Lyman graduated McGill College Montreal and studied further in England before being admitted to practice law in the Province of Quebec. He joined the family drug firm in 1829 at age fifteen and became a partner in Lyman Brothers — the largest pharmaceutical wholesaler in Canada — while also serving on Montreal City Council as Superintendent of West Ward and as Deacon in the Congregational Church.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sons died young, four to old age
narrative_blocks.body: Henry and Mary Corse Lyman had seven children together in Montreal between 1844 and 1861. Three died young: Arthur Ellis as an infant in 1847, Susan Alice at age five in 1853, and Roswell Corse at forty-two in 1892. The four surviving sons lived long lives, including Henry Herbert (who perished on the Empress of Ireland 1914) and Walter Ernest who reached eighty-nine.

burial.plot_notes: Ellis Cemetery, Huntington Hampshire County MA.

Ellis Cemetery, Huntington Hampshire County MA. Tombstone reads 'Died Jan. 29, 1836 aged 82' (Corbin Transcriptions Vol I Hampshire, NEHGS CD) but Corbin notes the transcribed year of 1856 may be the correct date — likely tombstone transcription error or weathering.

parents.father_research_notes: Samuel Ellis served in the French and Indian wars AND the Revolution per Edward Ellis MD's biographical notice. Both English-blood ancestors among the earliest New England colonists. Not built per Jalapeño Rule.

career.notes: Farmer in Norwich Massachusetts (region later renamed Huntington). Per Edward Ellis MD's 1885 biographical notice: 'his father was a farmer and the father of nine children, his five sons being each six feet or over in height.'

burial.plot_notes: Dawes Cemetery Cummington Hampshire Co MA — with second wife Sarah Moore Ellis.

career.notes: Farmer at Goshen Mass. per Edward Ellis Salisbury's 1885 family biographical compilation.

narrative_blocks.header: Twice-widowed farmer, four children across marriages
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Ellis was a farmer at Goshen and Cummington Massachusetts. He married first Betsey Kirkland in 1825, who died at age twenty-eight in 1832; they had twin children Lathrop Stiles and Luthena Elizabeth, both born February 14, 1828. He later married Sarah Moore of Worthington MA, with whom he had two more children — Jane Betsey (1835-1851, age sixteen) and John Holland (1839-1860, age twenty-one) — both of whom died young.

notable.notable_blurb: Meadville PA physician 59 years, Christ Church warden

education.notes: Attended school at Windsor Connecticut in 1818 (his mother Ruth Stiles Ellis's hometown).

education.school_name: Berkshire Medical College (then Medical Department of Williams College)
Post-graduate medical study, New York and Philadelphia
education.notes: Studied medicine in New York and Philadelphia after graduation.

career.notes: Came to Meadville 1826 (then a comparatively new and unsettled region) due to delicate health requiring a change of climate. For many years had to ride on horseback to all parts of Crawford County, largely through forests where 'blazed' trees (marked by the axe) were the only guides. Practiced for nearly 60 years until his death. The oldest practicing physician in Meadville at his death.
Organized the Crawford County Medical Society at Meadville PA in 1832 and served as its first President for many years.
Warden for many years; later Vestryman at Christ Church Meadville — where son-in-law Rev. Marison Byllesby served as Rector.

Largely interested in manufacturing and other enterprises which promised to contribute to Meadville's prosperity but proved unfortunate investments — resulted in total loss of his large property. His losses 'neither lessened the cheerfulness of his temper nor his kindness of heart' per History of Crawford County 1885.

narrative_blocks.header: Sixty-year Meadville physician, oldest at death
narrative_blocks.body: Dr. Edward Ellis came west to Meadville Pennsylvania in 1826 at age twenty-two — then a comparatively new and unsettled region — because of delicate health requiring a change of climate. For many years he rode on horseback to all parts of Crawford County, often through forests where blazed trees marked by the axe were his only guides. He practiced medicine there for nearly sixty years and was the oldest practicing physician in Meadville at his death in 1885.

narrative_blocks.header: Two wives died early, one daughter survived
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Ellis was twice widowed — his first wife Mary M. Kennedy died in 1840 after eight years of marriage, and his second wife Sarah Buchanan died in 1844 after only two years. From his second marriage came one daughter Elizabeth Ruth, who in 1864 married Rev. Marison Byllesby — then Rector of Christ Church Meadville, the same parish where Edward Ellis served as Warden and Vestryman.

sources.label: History of Crawford County Pennsylvania, Warner Beers & Co., 1885, page 736

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave #59156394. Stone: 'Harriet A / daughter of / Ebenezer & Ruth Ellis / Died March 16 1839 AE 30 ys'

naming_inspiration.description: Named in full for his maternal grandfather Ashbel Stiles. Variant spelling 'Ashbell' rather than 'Ashbel'.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Hannah Ellis Ellis (1812-1876), daughter of Ruth Stiles Ellis and Ebenezer Ellis; wife of her first cousin Noah Ellis.

burial.plot_notes: Eastman Cemetery, Chester Hampden Co MA — with husband Noah Ellis.

marriages.spouse_research_notes: Noah Ellis of Chester Massachusetts. Shared Ellis surname is coincidence — confirmed not cousins.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her first cousin Noah Ellis
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Ellis married Noah Ellis of Chester Massachusetts on January 14, 1839 at Huntington — and her husband was also her first cousin. Both descended from Samuel Ellis of Chester (1739-1826), a French and Indian War and Revolutionary War veteran: Hannah through her father Ebenezer; Noah through his father Noah Sr. Within-Ellis-family cousin marriages were a recurring pattern in the close-knit Hampden-Hampshire County hill towns of western Massachusetts.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for father Ebenezer Ellis; middle name Stiles honors mother's Stiles family lineage.

narrative_blocks.header: Chryste died young
narrative_blocks.body: Chryste died in 1831 at the age of twelve.

education.notes: Trained as physician (M.D.). Specific medical school undocumented.

career.notes: Practiced medicine briefly before his early death at age 30. Followed his older brother Edward (HD4235) into medicine.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Hooker honors his Thomas Hooker descent — the family progenitor. Sixth-generation descendant of the founder of Hartford.
